FT_IC1.evidence: Large cross-sectional sample of 9- to 11-year-old U.S. youth (N=11,875) from the Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development study, baseline data collected Sept 1 2016 - Nov 15 2018

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using negative binomial regression to examine associations of movement behavior recommendations with internalizing and externalizing behaviors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health

FT_IC2.evidence: Recreational screen time exposure is a composite bundling TV watching, video watching, video game playing, texting, social media, and video chat with no per-modality breakdown isolating phone or SM. Multi-activity composite without phone/SM isolation does not qualify.

notes: Paper meets IC1-IC6 but fails IC2 because the screen time measure bundles multiple devices and activities without isolating phone/SM specifically.

FT_IC1.evidence: Longitudinal study of 15,022 American children aged 9-11 from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study baseline

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using mixed-effects regression models to test association between family income and children's screen time

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Information journal

FT_IC2.evidence: Family income is the independent variable and screen time is the dependent variable. The other side of the SM relationship (family income) is demographic/SES-only, not an individual-trait-level variable (behavioral, health, neural, cognitive, well-being). Criteria explicitly exclude demographic-only predictors.

notes: Paper meets IC1-IC6 but fails IC2 because the predictor is family income (demographic), not an individual-trait-level variable like mental health, behavior, or cognition.

FT_IC1.evidence: National sample of 11,670 U.S. participants aged 9-10 years from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study, baseline and 1-year follow-up data

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using generalized additive mixed models to examine associations between sleep disturbances and mental health

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper examines associations between sleep disturbance/sleep duration (IVs) and depression/internalizing/externalizing (DVs). No smartphone or social media use variable appears as IV or DV in any analysis. Screen time is included as a covariate only ("total screen time was significantly associated with mental health score, including it did not result in a significant increase in model fit") but is not part of the primary or secondary focus. Does not qualify because there is no qualifying SM/phone variable.

notes: Paper meets IC1-IC6 but fails IC2 because it does not analyze any smartphone or social media use variable as IV or DV with an individual-trait outcome. Sleep is the primary exposure, not SM/phone.
